question_title: What will be the value of "Secured Overnight Financing Rate" on 2026-05-14?
summary: SOFR on May 14, 2026 will almost certainly be very close to its current stable level around 3.65%. The Fed held rates at 3.50-3.75% on April 29, with no FOMC meeting until mid-June. SOFR is bounded within the Fed Funds target band and highly mean-reverting, with a stable regime mean of 3.647% and std of 0.0135 since April 16. May 14 is a normal mid-month business day with no special calendar effects. Monte Carlo simulation using an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck mean-reverting process with mixture scenarios (97% normal regime, 2% repo stress, 1% emergency cut) produces a tight distribution centered at 3.648.

factors.description: Fed Funds target unchanged at 3.50-3.75% through May 14 - no FOMC meeting until June
SOFR stable regime mean of 3.647 with std 0.0135 since April 16 (11 observations)
Latest SOFR value of 3.66 (April 30) slightly above regime mean - mean reversion expected
May 14 is a normal mid-month business day - no month-end/quarter-end repo stress
Small tail risk: repo market stress could spike SOFR (like April 15 spike to 3.72)
factors.conditional: If repo market stress occurs on May 14

factors.description: Very small tail risk: emergency rate cut given 4-member FOMC dissent and CNN report on Fed cutting bias
factors.conditional: If emergency rate action occurs before May 14

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**SOFR on May 14, 2026: ~3.65%** (very tight distribution)

**Key reasoning:**
- The Fed held rates at **3.50%-3.75%** on April 29, 2026 — no FOMC meeting until mid-June
- SOFR is **bounded within the Fed Funds target band** and highly mean-reverting
- Stable regime since April 16 shows mean of **3.647%** with daily std of only **0.0135%**
- May 14 is a normal mid-month business day — no special repo market calendar effects
- Latest SOFR reading: **3.66%** (April 30)

**Distribution (Monte Carlo, OU mean-reverting process):**
| Percentile | Value |
|---|---|
| P10 | 3.60% |
| P25 | 3.62% |
| P50 | 3.65% |
| P75 | 3.67% |
| P90 | 3.69% |

The distribution is appropriately tight because SOFR is mechanically constrained by the overnight repo market within the Fed's policy band, with strong mean reversion and no expected policy change in the 10-day forecast window.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) What will be the value of "Secured Overnight Financing Rate" on 2026-05-14?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43384)
[Federal Funds Target Range - Upper Limit (DFEDTARU) | FRED | St. Louis Fed](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DFEDTARU)
[Federal Funds Target Range - Lower Limit (DFEDTARL) | FRED | St. Louis Fed](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/DFEDTARL)
[Federal Funds Effective Rate (FEDFUNDS) | FRED | St. Louis Fed](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/FEDFUNDS)
resolution_criteria: Resolves to the value found on the FRED API for the series SOFR once the data is published.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will paginate through the data found at the API endpoint `https://api.stlouisfed.org/fred/series/observations?series_id=SOFR`. This endpoint includes the latest data for the series. The latest revised data will be used when the resolution is assessed. The datapoint matching 2026-05-14 will be used to determine the resolution of this question.

A datapoint matches if:
1. The series is updated daily and the date of the datapoint is within 1 day previous to the resolution date.
2. The series is updated weekly and the date of the datapoint is within 7 days previous to the resolution date.
3. The series is updated monthly and the date of the datapoint is within 31 days previous to the resolution date.

If the datapoint is clearly skipped, or no datapoint is found after 2 weeks of checking then the question will be annulled.

factors.description: Fed Funds target unchanged at 3.50-3.75% through May 14 (no FOMC meeting until June)
SOFR stable regime mean of 3.647 with std 0.0135 since April 16
Latest SOFR value of 3.66 (April 30) slightly above regime mean - will mean-revert
May 14 is a normal Thursday - no month-end/quarter-end repo market stress
Small tail risk: repo market stress could spike SOFR temporarily (like April 15 spike to 3.72)
Very small tail risk: emergency rate cut (1% scenario per CNN/FOMC dissent signals)

input.anchor: Stable regime mean of 3.647% (from FRED SOFR series, regime stats since April 16, 11 observations)
input.assessment: SOFR on May 14 will almost certainly be very close to its current level of 3.65-3.66. The Fed held rates at 3.50-3.75% on April 29, and the next meeting is not until mid-June. SOFR is bounded within the Fed Funds target range and highly mean-reverting, with a stable regime mean of 3.647. May 14 is a normal business day with no special calendar effects. The distribution should be tight - centered around 3.647 with ~80% CI of about 3.60-3.69. Tail scenarios include repo market stress (upside) and emergency rate cut (downside), both very unlikely in a 10-day window.
input.calibration_notes: Distribution width calibrated against: (1) empirical stable-regime daily std of 0.0135, (2) full-month daily change std of 0.028, (3) observed April range 3.57-3.72. Final P10-P90 of 3.60-3.69 is consistent with expected behavior when no rate change occurs. Mean reversion ensures the distribution doesn't drift far from 3.65 over 8 business days.
input.key_uncertainties: Primary uncertainty is whether a repo market event or Treasury settlement could cause a temporary spike on exactly May 14. Secondary uncertainty is whether the small but non-zero emergency rate cut probability should be weighted differently given FOMC dissent signals.
input.tool_audit: FRED data via fetch_url: Excellent - provided full SOFR time series and regime stats. Web search: Useful for confirming FOMC meeting dates and rate decision. FRED Fed Funds target data: Confirmed 3.50-3.75 held steady. CNN article snippet: Informed about Fed signaling future cuts (asymmetric risk). Monte Carlo simulation: Ran 3 variants to calibrate distribution width.
input.process_reflection: This is an ideal question for the quantitative pipeline - SOFR is well-defined, has clean daily data, and the key driver (Fed Funds rate) is directly observable. The FRED API data was perfect. The main modeling challenge was calibrating the right width: too narrow ignores tail repo market events, too wide ignores the strong mean reversion. The OU process with mixture scenarios feels well-calibrated.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Verification Summary

**Resolution criteria**: ✓ Properly recovered and engaged with. The agent fetched the Metaculus question page, understood the FRED API resolution mechanism, and correctly identified that the datapoint for 2026-05-14 is what matters.

**Factor 1 (Fed Funds target unchanged)**: ✓ Fully supported by trace. DFEDTARU=3.75 and DFEDTARL=3.50 confirmed from FRED data. April 29 FOMC statement confirms rates held. The claim "no FOMC meeting until June" is based on reasonable inference from search results—no May meeting was identified, and FOMC meets every 6-8 weeks after the April 28-29 meeting.

**Factor 2 (SOFR stable regime mean)**: ✓ Directly confirmed by FRED SOFR series data in trace: stable_since: "2026-04-16", stable_mean: 3.647273, stable_std: 0.013545, observations_in_regime: 11.

**Factor 3 (Latest SOFR 3.66)**: ✓ Confirmed by trace: latest_value: 3.66, latest_date: "2026-04-30".

**Factor 4 (May 14 normal day)**: Minor error—agent says "Thursday" but May 14, 2026 is a Wednesday. However, the core claim (normal mid-month business day, no month-end/quarter-end effects) remains valid and doesn't affect the forecast.

**Factor 5 (repo stress tail)**: ✓ The April 15 spike to 3.72 is confirmed in the SOFR time series data.

**Factor 6 (emergency rate cut)**: ✓ The CNN article and Trading Economics snippet support the dissent signals; the 1% probability is the agent's judgment call, appropriately low.

**Double-counting check**: Factors 1 and 2 are related (SOFR is stable because the policy rate is stable) but draw on genuinely different evidence sources (policy rate data vs. empirical SOFR observations). Not a problematic duplication.

**Worldview consistency**: The archived worldview research on Fed policy (dated 2026-04-14) confirms rates at 3.50-3.75% with futures pricing no change through July 2026. It incorrectly predicted "no April meeting" but the agent's current research correctly supersedes this with the actual April 28-29 meeting outcome. No contradictory claims that would change the forecast.

**Monte Carlo approach**: The agent ran multiple simulation variants (OU mean-reverting, bootstrap, refined OU, and final mixture model). The final mixture model (97% normal + 2% repo stress + 1% emergency cut) produces reasonable percentiles: P10=3.60, P50=3.65, P90=3.69. This is well-calibrated for a rate bounded by the 3.50-3.75 target band with strong mean reversion.

**Probability assessment**: The center of 3.648 and range of 3.60-3.69 (approximate 80% CI) is consistent with the evidence. SOFR is strongly anchored by the Fed Funds band, no rate change is expected, and mid-month has no special calendar effects. I see no reason to meaningfully disagree with this estimate.

**Counterargument quality**: Strong—identifies specific weaknesses (11 data points, broader April range, 4-member dissent, Treasury issuance uncertainty) and constructs a coherent alternative with a wider 10-90 range.

**Confidence (0.72)**: Appropriate given small-sample regime stats and the possibility that model parameters could shift the tails.

**Tool audit**: Minor discrepancy—agent says "3 variants" of Monte Carlo but actually ran 4 code executions with simulations. This doesn't affect the forecast.
